SUMMARY:
While many studies focus on the impact of agriculture, especially livestock, on environmental outcomes (Detzel et al., 2022; Mosnier et al., 2021), fewer studies focus on the combined economic (Watson et al., 2017), nutritional (Gorissen et al., 2018; Draijer et al., 2023) and environmental impacts of the production of various protein sources. Focusing on protein production and corresponding land use, the boundaries of the analysis are set at the farm gate, as most of the land-use change drivers relate to farmers' and farms' characteristics (Heinze et al., 2022; Van Vliet et al., 2015). Proteins derived from plant-based products might be lower in quantity and in quality, but they are often less GHG-intensive than those derived from livestock (González et al., 2011).

A comparison of animal and plant-based proteins from an economic, environmental, and nutritional perspective in the Republic of Ireland

Highlights
----------

*   •The current protein production systems might not be able meet the growing demand for protein as well as climate targets. 
*   •This study evaluates and compares several protein sources in terms of economic, environmental and nutritional metrics. 
*   •The relative efficiency of the protein sources considered depends on the metric considered. 
*   •Land suitability for arable production is an important factor for any transition in production systems. 

Abstract
--------

### CONTEXT

Protein is a central component of health and nutrition. The current protein production system is unlikely to sustainably meet the growing global demand for protein. Therefore, alternative sources of protein must be considered.

### OBJECTIVE

This study uniquely compares animal-based (milk, beef meat, sheep meat) and plant-based (cereals and legumes) protein production in terms of land usage. The comparison is carried out across a suite of economic, environmental, and nutritional metrics. As land use decisions are taken at the farm level, the analysis adopts a farm gate approach. Focusing on the supply side, consumption is not considered in the analysis.

### METHODS

Economic performance is measured through the gross and net margins. Environmental performance is evaluated through farm-level greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions and nutrient (nitrogen and phosphorus) balances. Nutritional performance is measured through the gross protein yield, the protein yield corrected for digestibility and land-use efficiency, i.e., the land needed to generate one kg of (digestible) protein. Results are expressed per hectare and per 100 g of gross and digestible protein to allow for a holistic comparison. The analysis focuses on the Republic of Ireland, a relevant country given the importance of the strong agricultural export focus, the dominance of the livestock sector and the country's ambitious environmental emission reduction targets.

### RESULTS AND CONCLUSIONS

Findings indicate that, on a per hectare basis, milk production on dairy farms has the strongest economic performance and highest GHG emissions amongst the products considered. Crops' gross and net margins are less than half that of dairy farms, but they also show the strongest environmental performance generally. Cereals and legumes have the best nutritional performance, from a protein perspective, whereas sheep meat production returned the lowest gross and digestible protein yields per hectare. Arable crops also show the lowest GHG emissions and nutrient balances on a per unit of protein basis.

### SIGNIFICANCE

Land-use policies need to be designed holistically, given the complexity of the agricultural sector. Agricultural policy design currently focuses on income support and on the environmental impact of the agricultural sector, but farmers' livelihood and food and protein security need to be safeguarded. Diversifying agricultural production and increasing plant-based protein production in Ireland, a country with a livestock-focused agricultural sector, could contribute to achieving the climate change targets of the country, provided the necessary policy levers are in place.

Keywords

Protein production evaluation

Protein economic efficiency

Protein environmental efficiency

Agricultural land use

1. Introduction
---------------

In the next decade, population growth is expected to cause an increase in global meat, dairy and cereal consumption by 6.9%, 6.1% and 8.5% respectively ([OECD/FAO, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X24002932#bb0330)). A nutritious and affordable supply of food is unquestionably needed to meet this growing demand. However, the potential negative impact of food production on the environment remains one of the greatest global challenges. Proteins are a central component of health and nutrition; they contribute to key human physical processes, e.g., growth, development, maintenance ([FAO, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X24002932#bb0180)). On average, 37% of all protein consumed globally is derived from animal products. In high-income countries, this share goes up to 58% ([OECD/FAO, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X24002932#bb0330)). With livestock being a major contributor to global warming, protein production will become a central part of the challenge to sustainably feed the world's growing population.

A shift away from animal towards more plant-based protein production has several potential environmental benefits to meet the growing food and protein demand. However, the profitability at farm level of such a shift needs to be considered if land use change is to occur. The nutritional impact of substituting plant for animal-based proteins also needs to be considered, as consuming plant proteins may not have the same biological and health-promoting attributes as consuming animal-based proteins.

While many studies focus on the impact of agriculture, especially livestock, on environmental outcomes ([Detzel et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X24002932#bb0080); [Mosnier et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X24002932#bb0310)), fewer studies focus on the combined economic ([Watson et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X24002932#bb0435)), nutritional ([Gorissen et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X24002932#bb0220); [Draijer et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X24002932#bb0110)) and environmental impacts of the production of various protein sources. This study aims to compare several types of proteins using economic, environmental and nutritional metrics. Focusing on protein production and corresponding land use, the boundaries of the analysis are set at the farm gate, as most of the land-use change drivers relate to farmers' and farms' characteristics ([Heinze et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X24002932#bb0250); [Van Vliet et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X24002932#bb0420)). External factors such as processing requirements, technological or societal aspects vary across products; however, we assume the most influential factors related to land use decision occur inside the farm gates. As we focus on the supply side of the agricultural sector and related land-use decisions, consumers preferences are outside the scope of the analysis. Although micronutrients (e.g., zinc, iron) are important nutritional factors, the nutritional analysis of this paper focuses on protein quantity and quality, given the current protein production deficit observed in Ireland and Europe and that is currently making the region dependent on imports ([European Commission, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X24002932#bb0140)).

This paper then examines the following research question: how does plant-based protein production compare with animal protein production on a suite of economic, environmental, and nutritional metrics? The analysis focuses on milk, beef, sheep meat, cereals and legumes production. To allow a comparison in terms of land-usage, indoor based systems were not considered. We also investigate the differences in the distribution of sustainability performances across farm systems. Land-usage and protein production performances are also further analysed considering soil quality.

The paper is structured whereby the next section provides a contextual background to the research question, including a review of relevant literature and knowledge gaps. The third section presents the methodology and data used, while results are presented in the fourth section. In the concluding section of the paper policy implications are explored.

2. Literature review
--------------------

Meat consumption patterns have been compared to an environmental Kuznet curve, where consumption increases for low- and middle-income countries, but remains constant or decreases for high-income countries ([Cole and McCoskey, 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X24002932#bb0050)). For developed countries, this is due to many factors including the mixed health effects linked to meat consumption, animal welfare concerns, and concerns about the environmental impact of meat production and consumption ([Parlasca and Qaim, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X24002932#bb0335)). However, meat consumption also has a cultural component in middle-high income countries and can be related to crucial socio-economic characteristics such as income, power or status ([Leroy and Praet, 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X24002932#bb0285)). [Hundscheid et al. (2022)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X24002932#bb0260) identified the importance of the cultural status of meat as one of the main barriers to the sustainable protein transition that would involve reducing meat consumption and increasing plant-based protein consumption.

On the environmental side, several comparisons have been conducted between selected sources of animal and plant-based proteins. There is a significant gap between the carbon footprint of proteins derived from livestock products and that of plant-based proteins. For example, per 54 kcal, the CO 2 emissions from cow milk was more than 3 times higher than that of the plant-based substitutes ([Detzel et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X24002932#bb0080)). Cereals and legumes delivered between 56 and 505 g of protein per kg of CO 2 equivalent, against between 7.1 and 67 g for meats, dairy and eggs ([González et al., 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X24002932#bb0215)). In terms of nutrients, arable farms were found to have relatively low nitrogen surplus per hectare and high nitrogen use efficiency compared to dairy and pig farms ([Quemada et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X24002932#bb0350)). There are also disparities in the environmental impact across livestock systems. For example, mixed dairy-suckler systems have a higher energy consumption and GHG emissions per hectare than specialized cattle or specialized dairy systems. Specialized dairy systems also show lower energy consumption and GHG emissions per kg of protein than mixed and cattle specialized systems ([Diakité et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X24002932#bb0085)). Beef production from beef herds contributes more to acidification and eutrophication than lamb and mutton production, while beef production from dairy herds contributes more than beef production from beef herds [Poore and Nemecek, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X24002932#bb0340)). Pulses and grains have a lower impact on acidification and eutrophication than beef, but a somewhat comparable impact to that of lamb and mutton production ([Poore and Nemecek, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X24002932#bb0340)). On top of the provision of protein, livestock and plant-based products also provide micronutrients to the body, and at different environmental costs. For example, a Finnish study showed that emissions in terms of CO 2 equivalent per 100 g of iron were 10 times lower for broad beans than for beef. Emissions per 100 g of zinc were 50 times lower for mutton compared to peas ([Saarinen et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X24002932#bb0370)). However, this study did not account for the differences in bioavailability of nutrients between animal-based and plant-based protein sources. For example, haem iron, present in meat and fish, is more easily absorbed by the body than non-haem iron, present in pulses, cereals, fruits and vegetables ([FAO/WHO, 2004](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X24002932#bb0190)). The absorption of zinc was also twice as high from non-vegetarian diets compared to diets based on wheat and rice ([FAO/WHO, 2004](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X24002932#bb0190)).

Concerns regarding the sustainability of livestock production also include the rise in competition between feed and food production. A study across 16 European beef farms found that only one of them produced more human edible energy than it consumed ([Mosnier et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X24002932#bb0310)). However, some livestock systems can be efficient converters of non-edible matter into edible food. [Mottet et al. (2017)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X24002932#bb0315) found that ruminants systems globally were net producers of human-edible protein, needing 0.6 kg of human-edible protein feed to produce 1 kg of human-edible protein. However, monogastric systems were net consumers of human-edible protein, needing 2 kg of human-edible protein feed to produce 1 kg of human-edible protein.

While the studies cited above compared food products on their environmental impacts, it is also important to compare the nutritional value of the products. Three important metrics used to evaluate the nutritional aspects of protein are its protein content, amino acid content and amino acid digestibility ([Day et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X24002932#bb0070)). Amino acids are the molecules that compose proteins. The quality of a protein source can be determined by evaluating the digestibility of its composing amino acids. Animal protein sources are generally richer in essential amino acids, i.e., amino acids that cannot be synthesized by the human body, compared to plant-based proteins ([FAO, 1970](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X24002932#bb0170)). Proteins derived from plant-based products might be lower in quantity and in quality, but they are often less GHG-intensive than those derived from livestock ([González et al., 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X24002932#bb0215)). The EAT-Lancet report ([The Lancet Commissions, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X24002932#bb0405)) proposed a sustainable diet that encompasses the Paris Agreement's main target, i.e., “limit global warming to well below 2, preferably to 1.5 degrees Celsius, compared to pre-industrial levels” ([UNFCCC, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X24002932#bb0415)) by reducing meat and animal products consumption. The report recommended that global meat consumption be halved globally and divided by 3 in Europe and central Asia. It also recommended that legume consumption be more than doubled globally and multiplied by 5 in Europe and central Asia. However, the EAT-Lancet blueprint diet was subject to criticism since its publication, especially in relation to the recommended reductions in animal-products consumption. One of the main caveats is the deficiency of nutrients such as iron, zinc, vitamin B12 or calcium, in the EAT-Lancet blueprint diet ([Beal et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X24002932#bb0020)). When it comes to micronutrients, plant and animal-based protein again do not show the same quantity of e.g. zinc, iron or calcium. For example, milk is by far the largest provider of calcium, with 120 mg per 100 g of pasteurized milk against less than 30 mg for wheat, barley, beans or beef. Plant-based food are good providers of magnesium, with 24 and 34 mg per 100 g for beans and peas respectively against e.g., 11 mg per 100 g for milk. Peas and lean beef show similar amounts of iron, with 2.8 and 2.7 mg per 100 g respectively, but beans or wheat show lower returns at 0.9 and 2.0 mg per 100 g respectively. However, plant-based food does not contain vitamin B12, unlike livestock products ([Public Health England, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X24002932#bb0345)). The EAT-Lancet diet also sparked concerns related to the impact of the diet on non-communicable diseases, whereby the reduction of such diseases associated with the diet was questioned ([Stanton, 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X24002932#bb0390)). This shows that the nutritional comparison of animal-based protein and plant-based protein sources is not clear-cut and is subject to ongoing discussions.

The need for a holistic approach when it comes to agricultural production systems assessment has been previously highlighted ([Talukder et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X24002932#bb0395); [Robling et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X24002932#bb0360)). The Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations published guidelines regarding the inclusion of environmental and nutritional components when evaluating agricultural and food systems ([McLaren et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X24002932#bb0295)). Here, we focus on land-use and land-use decisions occurring at the production stage, hence the analysis concentrates on raw material production and characteristics. Including nutritional considerations can help inform policy decisions since the agricultural sector's primary aim is to guarantee food security. When it comes to land use and protein production, most studies rarely account for economic, environmental and nutritional dimensions simultaneously. [Table A.1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X24002932#t0030) (Appendix A) reviews indicators from studies which have assessed different elements of protein performances from an economic, environmental or nutritional perspective. Overall, most comparisons between animal and plant-based proteins focus on the quantity of protein. Some studies look at protein quality, but very few studies combine both aspects, and fewer still include economic and environmental considerations. This paper then aims to address this need by combining the abovementioned considerations to compare different land-based protein production systems at the farm level.

Several methods exist to determine the quality of proteins ([Adhikari et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X24002932#bb0005)). Here, we use the Digestibility Indispensable Amino Acid Score (DIAAS), promoted by the FAO as the preferred metric ([FAO, 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X24002932#bb0175)). For each indispensable amino acid, it compares the amount of amino acid digested by the body with the reference intake requirement of the amino acid. The lowest value is considered as the final score. This method encompasses several aspects of protein quality evaluation: amino acid content, digestibility and first limiting amino acid (amino acid present in the least amount compared to the reference requirements). DIAAS values are generally higher for animal-based protein sources than for plant-based sources, indicating a higher quality and digestibility of the amino acids for the former group ([FAO, 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X24002932#bb0175)).

3. The Irish agricultural and protein sector
--------------------------------------------

Data from the Republic of Ireland (Ireland henceforth) are used to address the research question. Although a small country, Ireland is a very relevant case-study for this research due to the size of its agricultural sector, the dominance of livestock-based agriculture over plant-based production systems and the significant volume of agri-food exports from Ireland each year. In 2021, Ireland was the eighth largest exporter of boneless cattle meat globally ([FAO, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X24002932#bb0185)). In 2020, 10.8% of the total beef production, 14.2% of the total sheep meat production and 5.9% of the total milk production in the European Union originated from Ireland ([Eurostat, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X24002932#bb0160)), despite accounting for only 2.8% of the gross domestic product of the Union ([Eurostat, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X24002932#bb0165)). Ireland is also the 11th largest exporter of bovine meat and 13th largest exporter of milk globally ([FAOSTAT, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X24002932#bb0195)). As such Ireland can be considered as an important contributor to global protein security. The large role played by Ireland in the European livestock sector compared to its relatively small economic size in the region makes it an interesting candidate for the comparative analysis performed in this study. Furthermore, the agricultural sector (dominated by livestock systems) is Ireland's largest single contributor of economy wide GHG emissions at approximately 37% in 2022 ([EPA, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X24002932#bb0120)), against 11% for the European average ([EEA, 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X24002932#bb0145)). To meet the national targets set out in the Irish Government's Climate Action Plan 2023, agricultural emissions have been mandated to decrease by 25% by 2030 compared to 2018 reference levels ([Government of Ireland, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X24002932#bb0230)). According to the latest projections for 2030, said emissions would be reduced by only 4% with existing measures and by almost 20% with additional measures ([EPA, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X24002932#bb0120)). Additional measures include agricultural efficiency measures (e.g., improved animal genetics, animal health enhancement), agricultural mitigation measures (e.g., reduced age of finishing, liming), land-use mitigation strategies (e.g., afforestation, agroforestry) and energy measures (e.g., biomethane, biomass crop production) ([Lanigan et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X24002932#bb0280)).

Abstract
--------

Dietary proteins are indispensable to human nutrition. In addition to their tissue-building function, they affect body composition and regulate various metabolic pathways, as well as satiety and immune system activity. Protein use can be examined from a quantitative or qualitative viewpoint. In this scoping review, we compare animal- and plant-based protein sources in terms of their effects on human health and the environment. We conclude that the consumption of vegetable protein sources is associated with better health outcomes overall (namely, on the cardiovascular system) than animal-based product use. The healthier outcomes of vegetable protein sources dovetail with their lower environmental impact, which must be considered when designing an optimal diet. Indeed, the health of the planet cannot be disjointed from the health of the human being. Future research will clarify the mechanisms of action underlying the health effects of plant-based protein sources when compared with animal sources, fostering better agronomic practices and influencing public health in a direction that will benefit both the planet and its inhabitants.

**Keywords:** protein sources, protein quality, main chronic diseases, vegetarian diets, environmental impact, climate change

1. Introduction
---------------

Dietary proteins are indispensable to human nutrition. Their most important biological function is tissue-building. However, proteins also affect body composition and regulate various metabolic pathways, as well as satiety and immune system activity [[1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9741334/#B1-nutrients-14-05115),[2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9741334/#B2-nutrients-14-05115)].

In the field of nutrition, protein consumption can be examined from a quantitative or qualitative viewpoint. Regarding the former, a recommended daily allowance (RDA) has been set at 0.8 g/kg of body weight (bw) for healthy adults under normal conditions [[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9741334/#B3-nutrients-14-05115)], but this varies depending on some factors, e.g., age, some physiological needs, pregnancy, etc. For example, athletes should likely increase their RDA to 1.2–1.6 g/kg of bw [[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9741334/#B3-nutrients-14-05115)].

In terms of their qualitative profile, it is important to analyze protein quality, i.e., a parameter that categorizes proteins into two groups on the basis of their amino acid composition: high- and low-quality proteins. In lay terms, this distinction is often referred to as animal- and plant-based proteins, respectively.

Another important factor that modulates the contribution of proteins to human health is the overall profile of protein-containing foods: the Acceptable Macronutrient Distribution Range (AMDR) considers the optimal range of protein intake to be between 10 and 35% of energy intake. Excluding dried edible insects, which can contain up to 60 g of protein per 100 g of product [[4](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9741334/#B4-nutrients-14-05115)], and some other sparsely consumed foods, such as soy protein isolate, stockfish, scraps of swine fat, and roe, the most commonly consumed foods rich in protein reach a maximum protein content of between 33 g and 37 g per 100 g of product. Some notable examples are dried soya and the flour derived from it, Parmesan cheese, cured meat, and guinea fowl [[5](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9741334/#B5-nutrients-14-05115)]. For this reason, it should be more appropriate to talk about “protein sources” rather than “proteins”.

High-income countries currently consume too many animal-based products and too few plant-based ones. This imbalance is thought to contribute to an increase in the risk of chronic diseases [[6](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9741334/#B6-nutrients-14-05115)].

For this reason, nutritional guidelines emphasize the need to shift toward plant-based diets (though not necessarily vegetarian diets). These recommendations dovetail with the excessive pressure that animal-based food systems exert on the planet. The syndemic relationship between chronic diseases and climate change is the subject of active research [[7](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9741334/#B7-nutrients-14-05115)] and is crucial to understanding the overall effects of the consumption of protein sources on human health and the environment.

In this scoping review, we analyzed the main food groups classified as protein sources [[8](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9741334/#B8-nutrients-14-05115)] and segregated them into the two macro-categories of “animal protein sources” and “plant-based protein sources”.

This analysis was carried out with priority given to the assessment of the direct effects of such foods on human health; consequently, correlations between their consumption and the most common chronic diseases were evaluated.

In addition, the environmental impacts of these foods have also been investigated with the aim of returning a more complete picture of the implications of different protein sources.

In evaluating this intricate picture, a brief but punctual description of some topics related to protein sources, such as protein quality, vegetarian and plant-based dietary patterns, and the double food pyramid of the Mediterranean Diet, is also reported.

2. Materials and Methods
------------------------

We searched the most popular databases (PubMed, Google Scholar, WebScience, Cochrane Library, and Scopus) for the most recent publications and reviews on this topic, using “plant-based protein” or “plant-based protein sources”, “vegetable protein” or “vegetable protein sources”, and “animal protein” or “animal protein sources” as keywords. We extracted the resulting publications, which were read, interpreted, and discussed among the authors.

For the topic of protein quality and the double food pyramid, we drew from various narrative reviews, some consensus papers, and systematic reviews; for the analysis of the vegetarian and Mediterranean dietary patterns, we used studies similar to the previous ones, as well as meta-analyses of prospective cohort studies and umbrella reviews, to assess the association between these two dietary patterns and chronic diseases. The latter types of papers were used for the creation of tables on individual protein sources, whereas interventional studies (i.e., randomized controlled trials, RCTs) were not considered.

We built tables as follows: the dark-gray background stands for a lack of studies (no data); the gray background (with a slash included in cells) indicates an absence of significance; light gray indicates a “nearly significant” association (inverse or positive); all other data with a white background illustrate statistically significant associations (inverse or positive).

3. Dietary Protein Quality
--------------------------

To compare animal and plant-based protein sources from the viewpoint of both nutrition and sustainability, it is useful to start from the concept of “protein quality” [[9](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9741334/#B9-nutrients-14-05115)]. There is a current debate on the need to revise and broaden this notion: in lay terms, “quality” is almost always associated with the idea of “desirability”, and therefore, its use in this context may be inappropriate and lead to misinterpretations [[10](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9741334/#B10-nutrients-14-05115)].

In describing protein quality, different approaches may be taken. The most traditional approach is to consider the positive biochemical impact of dietary proteins on protein synthesis and nitrogen balance [[11](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9741334/#B11-nutrients-14-05115)], An alternative approach is to consider the impact of dietary proteins on the function and metabolism of specific organs and/or hormones (e.g., evaluating the regulation of body composition and bone health, gastrointestinal (GI) function and bacterial flora, glucose homeostasis, cell signaling, satiety) [[12](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9741334/#B12-nutrients-14-05115)]. The most recent and far-reaching approach is to consider the net effects of diet on human health and the environment [[10](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9741334/#B10-nutrients-14-05115)].

### 3.1. Traditional Approach to Protein Quality Evaluation

In the traditional approach, the quality of a given protein source can be defined as its ability to efficiently meet the requirements of both nitrogen and essential amino acids (EAAs), and it depends on two factors:

*   the specific amino acid composition (the intrinsic quality of proteins);

*   digestibility (the extrinsic quality of proteins) [[13](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9741334/#B13-nutrients-14-05115)].

Concerning the first component, EAAs are a key factor in determining the intrinsic quality of proteins, which is represented by the combination of the nine EAAs’ content.

If the dietary content of a single EAA is lower than the individual’s reference requirement, this specific deficiency limits the use of any other amino acid. Therefore, even if the total nitrogen intake level is adequate, the “limiting amino acid” prevents normal rates of protein synthesis and determines the nutritional value of the total protein (or nitrogen) content in the diet [[13](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9741334/#B13-nutrients-14-05115)]. This is, however, a theoretical and academic simplification, given that the evaluation of the daily diet is more complex, because it contains a mixture of different protein sources. It is possible to circumvent the “limiting amino acid” problem through the precise complementation of protein sources [[14](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9741334/#B14-nutrients-14-05115)] (see below).

The concept of “limiting amino acid” has led to the development of amino acid scoring and, consequently, to a reference amino acid scoring pattern. Although there are currently some uncertainties in establishing this system, it is useful for comparing the quality of different protein sources [[11](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9741334/#B11-nutrients-14-05115),[13](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9741334/#B13-nutrients-14-05115)].

For extrinsic protein quality, there are two processes to consider:

*   the digestion of proteins and the absorption of the constituent amino acids (so-called digestibility) [[9](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9741334/#B9-nutrients-14-05115)]. «Digestibility is defined as the difference between the amount of N ingested and excreted, expressed as a proportion of N ingested». Due to the processes of protein metabolization of the intestinal microbiota, it is more appropriate to consider ileal digestibility than the fecal digestibility [[15](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9741334/#B15-nutrients-14-05115)]. More precisely, it is necessary to measure the true ileal digestibility (TID), which also takes into account the endogenous protein losses (both basal and specific ones) [[15](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9741334/#B15-nutrients-14-05115)].

*   The utilization of the absorbed amino acids to support whole-body protein synthesis (so-called availability).

These two different processes constitute what can be defined as the bioavailability (or metabolic availability) of nutrients, in this specific case, that of amino acids from dietary protein sources. In 1991, a score was proposed to merge these two components and then to evaluate the protein quality quantitatively [[16](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9741334/#B16-nutrients-14-05115)]; this is the Protein Digestibility Corrected Amino Acid Score (PDCAAS, reference proteins are e.g., egg white or casein), whose formula is:

This score has some limits, such as: (1) the truncation of the score to the value of 100%, which does not allow for a comparison between proteins with very high scores [[17](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9741334/#B17-nutrients-14-05115)]; (2) the use of fecal digestibility rather than TID [[17](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9741334/#B17-nutrients-14-05115)]; (3) the restriction to only the first limiting amino acid [[17](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9741334/#B17-nutrients-14-05115)]; (4) the lack of consideration of the bioavailability of every single EAA [[12](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9741334/#B12-nutrients-14-05115),[18](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9741334/#B18-nutrients-14-05115)].

The latter issue is particularly important, since the relevance of considering amino acids as individual nutrients has recently been understood [[19](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9741334/#B19-nutrients-14-05115)].

In order to augment the PDCAAS, in 2011, the “FAO Expert Consultation on Protein Quality Evaluation in Human Nutrition” introduced another score, the Digestible Indispensable Amino Acid Score (DIAAS). DIAAS is calculated as follows [[18](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9741334/#B18-nutrients-14-05115)]:

According to this score, protein sources are classified into three categories [[18](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9741334/#B18-nutrients-14-05115)]: (1) “excellent” sources (DIAAS > 100%); (2) “good” sources (75% < DIAAS < 100%); and (3) “no claim” sources (DIAAS < 75%).

Because they generally contain reduced amounts of one or more EAAs, plant-based protein sources have lower DIAAS values (and, for that matter, PDCAAS values) and are therefore defined as “low-quality protein sources” [[20](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9741334/#B20-nutrients-14-05115)].

The term “incomplete protein sources” «is no longer commonly used as all protein sources contain all 20 amino acids» [[21](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9741334/#B21-nutrients-14-05115)].

However, plant-based protein sources are complementary to each other. The classic example of protein complementation is the combination of cereals and legumes (for simplicity, think of the Italian recipe of pasta and beans): legumes typically have low/limiting levels of methionine (a sulfur-containing amino acid) and high levels of lysine. Cereals have exactly the opposite characteristics [[20](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9741334/#B20-nutrients-14-05115)].

### 3.2. Recent Approaches to Protein Quality Evaluation

Because dietary proteins have more biological functions than the mere maintenance of body protein mass, the concept of protein quality has to be broadened in order to include new elements in a quantitative evaluation. To achieve this new “protein quality metric”, it is also necessary to consider intake levels that are higher than the RDAs as normal, at least in high-income countries [[12](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9741334/#B12-nutrients-14-05115)]. Such levels were established based only on the maintenance of the nitrogen balance in nearly all (97.5%) healthy individuals [[22](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9741334/#B22-nutrients-14-05115)]. At the same time, it is also necessary to consider the environmental impact of protein use. Of all the various factors that can be measured through the footprint methodology [[23](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9741334/#B23-nutrients-14-05115)], the main ones related to food systems could be conveniently classified as: (a) greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions (carbon footprint); (b) freshwater (or blue water) use (water footprint); (c) cropland use (land footprint or the wider ecological footprint); (d) cumulative energy demand (CED, carbon component of the ecological footprint); (e) the use of chemicals, such as fertilizers and pesticides (specific component of material footprint for fertilizers and specific component of chemical footprint for pesticides); (f) loss of biodiversity (biodiversity footprint).

A first attempt to harmonize such measurements was proposed by Katz et al. [[10](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9741334/#B10-nutrients-14-05115)] through the definition of two different sample metrics.

Yet, given the enormous complexity of the topic in terms of both nutrition and sustainability, multiple aspects must be evaluated [[10](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9741334/#B10-nutrients-14-05115),[14](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9741334/#B14-nutrients-14-05115)]. Currently, there are various approaches as to how the different factors should be considered. A pertinent example is provided by an Italian study, which assessed how the cropland use and GHG emissions of various protein sources should be measured [[24](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9741334/#B24-nutrients-14-05115)]. The urgency of developing an evidence-based tool in this specific area is well-highlighted in a rather recent article [[25](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9741334/#B25-nutrients-14-05115)], in which the Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) [[26](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9741334/#B26-nutrients-14-05115),[27](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9741334/#B27-nutrients-14-05115),[28](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9741334/#B28-nutrients-14-05115)] is proposed as the benchmark method of analysis.

4. Dietary Patterns and Protein Intake
--------------------------------------

The various dietary patterns can be conveniently differentiated according to various methods [[26](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9741334/#B26-nutrients-14-05115),[29](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9741334/#B29-nutrients-14-05115)]. An a priori index is derived from a series of dietary recommendations for a healthy and high-quality dietary model. The second method of dietary pattern assessment consists of “data-driven” approaches, such as cluster analysis and factor analysis, that are outcome-independent and derive a posteriori patterns.

The third method is based on individual preferences in food and beverage intake.

### 4.1. Vegetarian Dietary Patterns

Vegetarian dietary patterns are an emblematic example of the third assessment method, but they can also be characterized by the first a priori method through a more generic distinction in “plant-based diets”.

The various vegetarian dietary patterns differ based on their protein sources: from the semi-vegetarian diet (flexitarian) to the vegan one, animal meat (red meat, poultry, and seafood) and animal derivatives (eggs and dairy products) are progressively eliminated [[30](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9741334/#B30-nutrients-14-05115),[31](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9741334/#B31-nutrients-14-05115)]. Another slightly different definition of vegetarian patterns is based on the frequency (greater or less than one time per month) of the intake of different animal protein sources [[32](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9741334/#B32-nutrients-14-05115)]. However, several studies have shown that the differences in the intake of the various foods between the omnivorous diet and the various subtypes of vegetarian diets do not only concern animal protein sources.

In the cohorts of the Adventist Health Study 2 (AHS-2) [[33](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9741334/#B33-nutrients-14-05115),[34](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9741334/#B34-nutrients-14-05115)] and the European Prospective Investigation into Cancer and Nutrition (EPIC) (Oxford) [[35](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9741334/#B35-nutrients-14-05115)]—the two largest of the known ongoing prospective studies that include high proportions of vegetarians—and that of the National Health and Nutrition Examination Surveys (NHANES) 2013–2016 [[36](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9741334/#B36-nutrients-14-05115)], vegetarian dietary patterns are characterized by: (a) a greater intake of certain major food categories (clearly plant-based protein sources (legumes, seeds, and nuts) but also fruits, vegetables, whole grains and tubers) and of some micronutrients (fibers, minerals (magnesium, above all), and vitamins (A, C, and E types)) [[37](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9741334/#B37-nutrients-14-05115)]; (b) a lower intake of other food categories (refined cereals, saturated fatty acids (SFAs) of animal origin, added fats, sweets, snack foods, and non-water beverages) and of other micronutrients (cholesterol, sodium, vitamin B12, and zinc). Therefore, the differences in terms of health outcomes (see below) cannot be directly linked only to the different consumption of protein sources.

#### 4.1.1. Health Outcomes

The opening statement of the first edition of the “Dietary Reference Intakes for Energy, Carbohydrate, Fiber, Fat, Fatty Acids, Cholesterol, Protein, and Amino Acids” reported that «a varied vegetarian diet can get the same quality of protein and similar amounts of nitrogen as yielded by animal protein or a mixed diet» [[38](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9741334/#B38-nutrients-14-05115)]. Moreover, the US scientific report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee (DGAC) also stated that a “Healthy Vegetarian Pattern” is associated with health benefits [[26](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9741334/#B26-nutrients-14-05115)]. In addition, the Italian Society of Human Nutrition declared through a position paper that «well-planned vegetarian diets […] provide adequate nutrient intake» [[31](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9741334/#B31-nutrients-14-05115)]. A recent review has definitively dispelled the myth of the inadequacy of various vegetarian diets for protein and amino acids requirements [[39](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9741334/#B39-nutrients-14-05115)].

Much research has been performed to better understand the differences in health outcomes between omnivorous diets and vegetarian diets. # Balancing nutrition and nature: why cutting the animal protein in our diets has mixed environmental impacts

![](https://www.yogurtinnutrition.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/12/yinimodel_newpost_environmental.png)
![](https://www.yogurtinnutrition.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/04/1.svg)
![](https://www.yogurtinnutrition.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/04/2.svg)
![](https://www.yogurtinnutrition.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/04/3.svg)
![](https://www.yogurtinnutrition.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/04/4.svg)

**Shrinking the share of animal protein in our diet has become a focus for protecting both human and planetary health. But, while reducing animal protein is set to ease pressures on the environment, it could also come at a cost, latest research reveals (1).**

If not carefully designed, our low animal protein diet could spell bad news for the wealth of the world’s fauna and flora which could fall victim to the changing agricultural practices.

The findings, based on diet modelling, have led researchers to call for further research into the best balance of animal-sourced protein in our shift towards more sustainable, plant-based diets. The modern diet should take account of all influences on sustainable eating and may require radical changes in our agriculture, the researchers say.

## **Modelling sustainable diets for human and planetary health**

Environmental pressures created by the global food system are driven mainly by the high proportion of animal products in our diet (2). Reducing the share of animal-based foods we eat could therefore bring major benefits to the environment (3) and is a key target of food policies to increase sustainability (4,5). But because animal products are such an important source of protein and micronutrients, cutting down on them also risks making the diet less affordable or acceptable (6,7). Optimized diets may have difficulty to meet the requirement for certain nutrients (calcium, vit. B6 or B12, vit D, iodin), some of them being specific animal-sourced micro-nutrients.

Adding to this dilemma, a team of French scientists has shone a spotlight on the pros and cons for the environment of reducing animal protein in our diet (1).

They previously explored the minimum share of animal protein that met all food nutrient recommendation (8). For this new publication, using a national database of French adults’ diets, they developed five model low-animal-protein diets for different groups of adults based on gender and age. The observed diet is not fulfilling all nutrient recommendations, but these low-animal-protein diets contained the least animal protein needed to fulfil nutritional needs – around 50% of dietary protein – while minimising changes in the quantity and affordability of food consumed.

A low animal protein diet contains more fruits and vegetables (+103%), pulses, potatoes and unrefined grain products (+142%), more eggs (+96%), more dairy products but with variations within this category, with more milk (+222%), the same quantity of yogurt and less cheese (-97%) and of course, less meat –66%).

## **Tracking ecological impacts from field to fork**

The researchers then used a lifecycle assessment to compare the environmental impacts of model low-animal-protein diets with those of typical French observed diets, where around 70% of protein is from animal sources. This method tracked various ecological effects from ‘field-to-fork’, including farming, processing, packaging, transport, retail, consumer use, and waste disposal. Here’s what they found…

## **Cutting down on animal protein has positive effects on the environment**

Results suggested that reducing the share of animal protein from 70% to 50% of total protein intake could significantly ease several key environmental pressures. Differences between typical and low-animal-protein diets were similar for each of the five groups of adults studied:

## **Reducing animal protein can also have harmful environmental impacts**

On the down-side, the researchers uncovered some concerning trade-offs that could occur with low-animal-protein diets, particularly in water use and biodiversity:

## **How can we balance these mixed environmental impacts?**

The results of this modelling study suggest that cutting the share of animal protein we eat to 50% is compatible with nutritional needs, affordability and consumption constraints, but could have mixed effects on the environment. Therefore, any shift toward low-animal-protein diets should be carefully managed to balance these environmental trade-offs.

When designing sustainable diets, while covering all nutrient requirements of a population (taking into account age, gender and physical status specificities) it is important to consider all aspects of sustainability, the researchers say. In the modelling, they found that environmental benefits were driven by decreases in red meat consumption while introducing the concern on its impact on biodiversity. Increased consumption of fresh fruits, vegetables and fatty fish explained most environmental challenges related to water use.

The researchers propose that shifting the shares of plant and animal products in diets may require transforming agricultural practices and food systems to address concerns about climate change, biodiversity preservation and water consumption.

“While shifting toward a more plant-based diet is promoted, especially in Western countries, the optimal share of animal protein compatible with a sustainable diet has yet to be determined.”

enriched_points.expanded_content: Animal-based proteins, primarily from meat and dairy, are rich in essential amino acids but contribute significantly to health and environmental challenges, needing careful consumption.
Animal agriculture is a leading cause of greenhouse gas emissions, deforestation, and biodiversity loss, necessitating a shift toward more sustainable protein sources.
Producing animal proteins requires extensive land, water, and feed resources, making it less efficient compared to plant-based alternatives, which utilize fewer resources for protein production.
